channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: Marginalized Animals: Important Life Lessons from "Pests"
item.description:
Animal Behavior

Marginalized Animals: Important Life Lessons from "Pests"

Marlene Zuk's new book discusses unpopular creatures who have lots to teach us.

Posted March 17, 2026

Key points
  • Zuk repositions these species and offers a new appreciation for the animals we often shun.
  • Raccoons, rats, and other animal intruders teach us about intelligence, adaptability, and ourselves.
  • We learn why these unpopular creatures have something special to teach us about our own place in nature.
Source: Vincent M.A. Janssen/Pexels.

Many of the nonhuman animals (animals) with whom we share our homes and other landscapes are often mistakenly written off as being unimportant and disposable "pests" or "trash" animals.1 However, they deserve more respect, we kill them far too often, and writing them off says more about us than them, and these animals, including raccoons, rats, and coyotes, actually are very important neighbors.

For these and other reasons, we should all be thankful to animal behavior expert Dr. Marlene Zuk for offering valuable information about what these and other animal intruders can teach us about intelligence, adaptability, and ourselves in her new book Outsider Animals: How the Creatures at the Margins of Our Lives Have the Most to Teach Us.

Marc Bekoff: Why did you write Outsider Animals?

Marlene Zuk: I love most animals, including ones that no one has heard of, like tardigrades, or ones that make most people nervous, like spiders. And of course there are lots of books about popular animals like chimpanzees, cats, dogs (not to cast aspersion, Marc!) and even octopus. But I wanted to write a book about a different category, those animals that live on the margins, those that we encounter whether we want to or not, like gulls or coyotes or even cockroaches, and show people how much more there is to them than they might think.

MB: How does your book relate to your background and general areas of interest?

MZ: My research has always centered on the evolution of behavior, and I’ve also long been interested in how we see animal behavior as a reflection of our own, and vice versa – sometimes we see what animals do and say, “that’s just like a human!” and sometimes we look at animals and draw some lesson about what we should be doing from it. To my mind, both of those approaches have problems, because they prevent us from seeing the animals as they are, rather than as either robots or replicas of humans. As a biologist, I know that animals are much more than those options.

Source: Princeton University Press/with permission.

MB: Whom do you hope to reach?

MZ: Many people share my love of animals and nature, and I hope they check out the book. But other people – or maybe even a partly overlapping group – like some animals, but dismiss others as pests or intruders.

I want to reach both types, and maybe show them a side of animals that they haven’t seen before. Cockroaches, for example, can teach us something about human pregnancy, and rats help us think about what our laboratory animals really exemplify (hint: it isn’t as much as we think). Even cabbage white butterflies, which most people don’t think of as an invasive species, can challenge us to reconsider what it means to belong somewhere

article continues after advertisement

MB: What are some of the topics you consider and some of your major messages?

MZ: How do we live with animals in urban, suburban and even wild areas? It can be hard to navigate the edges, where both humans and animals lay claim. I want us to ask some hard questions about who belongs where, rather than reflexively assuming humans always have the upper hand or that we need to go back to a natural state, whatever that is. That means re-considering what we mean by invasive species. For the chapter on cabbage white butterflies, for instance, I interviewed butterfly expert Art Shapiro, who pointed out that many native butterfly species now depend on introduced plant species for food. Does that mean we should get rid of the plants and let the butterflies go extinct? Probably not. But there are few easy answers, and it’s important to recognize the complexity facing us.

Along those lines, I explore how people blame animals like raccoons or cowbirds or Common Mynas for damaging their property or harming other species that they happen to like more. Much of the time, it was humans who caused the problem to begin with; for example, if our trash wasn’t so omnipresent and accessible, raccoons wouldn’t find cities so inviting.

I also delve into the biology of each of the species in the book, showing how fascinating the lives of animals we thought we knew, like gulls or snakes, can be. Snakes may be the only animal that’s really changed human evolution, for instance, and gulls have illustrated the prevalence of same-sex pairing in the animal kingdom.

And I had a great time learning about the sometimes-inspiring and sometimes-kooky role the animals in the book play in our culture. I hope readers enjoy hearing about archy the cockroach (always in lower case), about Rascal the raccoon, and about snakes in literature from the Bible to J.K. Rowling.

MB: How does your work differ from others that are concerned with some of the same general topics?

MZ: Many books explore our relationship with animals, and I love a lot of those books. But some of them also seem to romanticize animals, perhaps hoping that if we are told that animals are smarter or cuter than we’d thought, we will love them more. I would rather have people see animals on their own terms, rather than claiming a kinship we don’t necessarily have or anthropomorphizing them. And we need to be realistic about animals – there are very real concerns about rats in urban settings, for example.

article continues after advertisement

At the same time, I think Outsider Animals asks people to reconsider some species they’ve traditionally dismissed – not many books extoll the virtues of cockroaches, for example! So I am also championing the underdog, or more accurately the under-bird or under-insect.

MB: Are you hopeful that as people learn more about these fascinating and important animals, they will treat them with more respect, compassion, and dignity?

MZ: Absolutely. It’s easy to only think of animals in terms of what they do for (or to) us. I hope people can look at them and appreciate them for themselves. Here I’m using the word “appreciate” the way people who take a wine appreciation or art appreciation class might. You don’t expect to come out of those courses loving every wine or every painting, but you do hope to gain more knowledge and understanding. I realize that many (most?) people won’t stop squashing cockroaches, but maybe, just maybe, they can see them as more than just pests.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Marc Bekoff Ph.D.
